steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage states that vocabulary accompanying crops as they spread is a global phenomenon, then pivots with “yet” to introduce a contradiction. The blank must supply evidence showing that corn vocabulary did NOT follow the crop into the Southeast — that is, data proving decoupling.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — the origins of vocabulary pertaining to the crop vary across languages in the region, with the words for corn in Cherokee and the Muskogean languages showing no demonstrable relationship to Totozoquean vocabulary.: directly proves the crop spread without its Totozoquean vocabulary, completing the “yet” contrast. ✓
  • Choice B — the region is linguistically diverse, being home not only to Chitimacha and Caddo, but also to the Muskogean language family (including Chickasaw, Choctaw, and Muscogee) and to one Iroquoian language (Cherokee).: catalogues regional languages but says nothing about the origin of corn-related vocabulary; does not complete the contrast. ✗
  • Choice C — corn-related vocabulary underwent changes when entering other, unrelated languages, as can be seen by the divergence of the Caddo word from the Chitimacha word it originated in.: shows vocabulary that did travel and transform, which supports the phenomenon the passage is contrasting against, not the exception. ✗
  • Choice D — words for corn in the languages of the Muskogean family evolved from a common root, with the Muscogee word having lost certain consonant sounds still present in the Chickasaw and Choctaw words.: describes internal relationships within Muskogean and says nothing about whether those words relate to Totozoquean vocabulary. ✗
A is correct.
